Figure 3.6a shows the molecular structure of a glucose molecule. A single line symbolizes a single covalent bond between atoms and a double line symbolizes a double covalent bond. Similarly, if there were a triple covalent bond between atoms present in this molecule, it would be symbolized by a triple line. In this example, the structure of glucose is shown in an open chain format which appears to exist as a two-dimensional structure. However, it is important to note that in reality, molecules and compounds exist as three-dimensional structures. Furthermore, many compounds are also formed in special structural shapes. For example, the majority of glucose molecules in our bodies are stored in a ring-like structure comprising a ring-shaped 'carbon skeleton' with hydroxyl groups attached (hydroxyl groups consist of an oxygen and carbon atom joined by a single covalent bond) (see Figure 3.6b). Given that organic molecules and compounds are often relatively big, we sometimes draw their constitutional formula using shorthand methods (see Figure 3.6c).

![a. A molecular structure of glucose molecule. b. A molecular structure of glucose. c. A molecular structure of glucose.](Attachments/Books/biochemistry-for-sport-and-exercise-maclaren/c03f006.png)

## Definition

A molecular structure diagram is a graphical representation of a molecule or compound that depicts its constituent atoms and the covalent bonds between them. Glucose serves as a representative example, illustrated in both open-chain and ring-form structures.

## Core Principles

- A single line between atoms symbolizes a single covalent bond.
- A double line symbolizes a double covalent bond.
- A triple line symbolizes a triple covalent bond.
- Although structural diagrams are commonly drawn in two dimensions, real molecules and compounds exist as three-dimensional structures.
- Many compounds adopt special structural shapes (e.g., ring forms), which are biologically relevant.
- A hydroxyl group consists of an oxygen atom and a hydrogen atom joined to a carbon by a single covalent bond.

## Sub-concepts

- **Open-chain structure**: A linear depiction of a molecule, as shown for glucose in Figure 3.6a.
- **Ring structure**: A closed 'carbon skeleton' shape with functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl) attached; the majority of glucose molecules in the body are stored in this ring-like form (Figure 3.6b).
- **Shorthand / constitutional formula**: A simplified notation used because organic molecules and compounds are often relatively large (Figure 3.6c).
- **Hydroxyl group**: A common functional group attached to the carbon skeleton in carbohydrates such as glucose.

## Field-level Controversies

## 文獻分歧 / Discussion

## Practical Applications

- Interpreting biochemical diagrams of carbohydrates and other organic molecules in sport and exercise biochemistry.
- Recognising the storage form of glucose (ring structure) when studying metabolism and energy substrates.
- Using shorthand constitutional formulas to efficiently represent and communicate the structures of large organic molecules.

- Glucose
- Carbon skeleton
